# Raw research: Gender, diversity, and movement leadership (prompt 19)

**Prompt:** [docs/build/research/prompts/19-gender-diversity-movement-leadership.md](../prompts/19-gender-diversity-movement-leadership.md)  
**Date:** 2026-04-12  
**Methods:** Web retrieval (Publishers Weekly, Pew, Barna summaries), web search synthesis, internal argument IDs referenced in the prompt (`research-049`, `research-003`, `research-016`, `research-017`, `C-AU01`). No primary statistical audit of Movemental’s 146-row scoring sheet was performed in this pass.

---

## 0. Internal anchor (from prompt)

- **Movemental argument index:** Women are **18%** of **146** scored movement-leader candidates — framed as a **gender gap in visibility and network** (`research-049`). Treat downstream analysis as **conditional on that internal count** until re-verified against the live dataset export.

---

## 1. Gender in U.S. Christian leadership (public statistics)

### 1.1 Congregational leadership (Hartford / National Congregations Study lineage)

- Secondary reporting on Hartford Institute / National Congregations Study-type figures often cites on the order of **~10–12%** of U.S. congregations with a **woman as senior or sole ordained leader**, with **higher** shares in **mainline** contexts and lower shares in many evangelical/conservative contexts.  
- **Caveat:** Exact year and survey wave matter; use Hartford’s **Fast Facts** and NCS documentation for any public-facing number: https://hirr.hartfordinternational.edu/fast-facts-on-american-religion/

### 1.2 Barna — Protestant senior pastors (trend, not 2025 headline)

- Barna has published trend work showing **female senior pastors in Protestant churches increased** from historically very low levels (e.g. **5% in the early 1990s**; **10% by 2009** in one widely cited Barna summary). See: https://www.barna.com/research/number-of-female-senior-pastors-in-protestant-churches-doubles-in-past-decade/  
- Barna also notes **denominational concentration**: a large share of female senior pastors are in **mainline** traditions vs evangelical spaces (same article summarizes distribution).

### 1.3 Pew — top leadership rarity + ordination divides

- Pew **Fact Tank (2016)** summarizes that **women remain relatively rare in top positions** of major U.S. religious bodies, with additional context on which traditions ordain women and how rarely women reach denominational apex roles: https://www.pewresearch.org/fact-tank/2016/03/02/women-relatively-rare-in-top-positions-of-religious-leadership/  
- Older Pew reporting (2014) on **congregations led by women (~11% in 2012, flat vs 1998** in one national sample) is useful historically but **should not be quoted as “current”** without checking for updated waves: https://www.pewresearch.org/fact-tank/2014/09/09/the-divide-over-ordaining-women/

**Implication for interpreting 18%:** Depending on how “movement leader” is defined (ordained senior leader vs author/speaker/practitioner founder), **18% may be roughly in line with parts of U.S. Protestant leadership data** or **better than senior-pastor percentages in some conservative slices** — but it can still be **worse than Movemental’s stated aspirations** for a curated credibility graph if the product story implies representative breadth.

---

## 2. Christian publishing and visibility (workforce + gatekeeping)

### 2.1 ECPA baseline DEI survey (2022; reported 2023)

- **Publishers Weekly** (2023-05-09), “Christian Publishers Measure a DEI Baseline,” summarizes an **Evangelical Christian Publishers Association** workforce survey: https://www.publishersweekly.com/pw/by-topic/industry-news/religion/article/92235-christian-publishers-measure-a-dei-baseline.html  
- Key figures (among participating publishers; **not a census**):
  - Workforce **gender:** **56% men / 44% women** in religion publishing vs **~77% women** in general publishing (per PW’s comparison framing).
  - **Executive/senior managers:** **11% men vs 4% women** in those buckets as reported (PW phrasing: men more likely to occupy executive/senior manager roles).
  - **Participation caveat:** Only **~26% of 70 ECPA members** participated; reported **margin of error ~±20%** after adjustment — treat as **directional baseline**, not precision engineering.

### 2.2 Author-market narrative (non-rigorous but directionally informative)

- PW and trade press describe **growth in women-authored religious titles** and expanded women-focused imprints (market response story). Useful for “visibility is improving in one channel” — still compatible with **concentrated gatekeeping** at executive/editorial levels per ECPA baseline.

---

## 3. Pipeline vs selection bias — conceptual anchors

### 3.1 “Invisible labor” / credited visibility (organizational sociology)

- **Joan Acker**, “Hierarchies, Jobs, Bodies: A Theory of Gendered Organizations,” *Gender & Society* (1990) — canonical argument that organizations embed **implicit male-bodied “ideal worker”** templates; jobs and evaluation criteria reproduce advantage. DOI: `10.1177/089124390004002002`  
- Later Acker work on **inequality regimes** helps frame how **multiple criteria** (publication, institutional role, movement metrics) can **interlock** rather than each being “neutral” in practice.

### 3.2 Gendered patterns in publication, citation, speaking (general science + org behavior)

- Broad literatures (not Christianity-specific) document **citation gaps**, **credit gaps**, **invitation gaps** for conference speaking, and **network homophily** in hiring/recommendation. Movemental-relevant translation: **“visible movement impact”** often tracks **public artifacts** (books, stages, titles) that are themselves **gender-skewed upstream**.

---

## 4. Network science / diversity-of-cognition (careful use)

### 4.1 Hong & Page — diversity in problem-solving groups

- Hong, L., & Page, S. E. (2004). “Groups of diverse problem solvers can outperform groups of high-ability problem solvers.” *PNAS*, **101**(46), 16385–16389. DOI: `10.1073/pnas.0403723101`  
- **Claim type:** Formal / computational argument about **diversity of heuristics** improving search in certain problem classes — **not** a moral theorem about fairness and **not** automatically transferable to “social identity diversity ⇒ better trust graph” without careful mapping.

### 4.2 Granovetter — weak ties; Burt — structural holes

- **Weak ties** and **structural holes** literature supports the general point that **network brokerage** and **non-redundant information** matter for opportunity and innovation — useful background for why a **homogeneous endorsement graph** can **miss pockets of legitimate movement leadership** even when average “scores” look fine.

### 4.3 Homophily / cumulative advantage in network formation

- Sociological work on **homophily** and **preferential attachment** supports the product concern: **early composition** shapes **later invitations**, especially in **small-N curated** systems (see prompt Q9).

---

## 5. Comparable curated platforms (illustrative, mixed rigor)

### 5.1 TED (public reporting + secondary analysis)

- Media summaries of academic work report **rising** women’s share among TED speakers over long horizons, with **historical underrepresentation** and persistent issues in other dimensions (e.g. ethnicity). Example secondary writeup: https://phys.org/news/2019-09-women-ted-ethnic-minorities.html (describing a study of TED.com talks 2006–mid-2017).  
- **Use constraint:** treat as **analogy**, not as proof of what Movemental “should” be.

### 5.2 Masterclass / YPO / GLN

- **No stable public denominator** for “curated expert gender %” was retrieved in this pass without paywalled reports — recommend **manual desk research** if Movemental wants hard comparables for investor decks.

---

## 6. Theological / movement-practice notes (high level)

### 6.1 APEST / mDNA framing

- **Alan Hirsch’s fivefold (APEST) framing** is not inherently gendered in text, but **historical apostolic imagery and practice communities** can skew masculine in lived culture; any platform using **network-first discovery** inherits **who was historically platformed** in those networks.

### 6.2 Egalitarian movement voices (prompt examples)

- Leaders named in the prompt (e.g. **Felicity Dale**, **Jo Saxton**, **Tina Teng-Henson**) are useful **counterexamples** of visible women in movemental / fivefold-adjacent spaces — useful for **spot-checking whether scoring criteria surface them** vs only more institutionally legible male peers.

---

## 7. Open questions for a follow-up internal pass

1. **Recompute** gender coding on the 146 scored candidates with an explicit methodology note (self-ID vs inferred vs declined).  
2. **Stratify** 18% by tradition bucket (mainline / evangelical / charismatic / global South diaspora networks) — raw headline may hide **excellent** representation in one slice and **poor** in another.  
3. **Sensitivity test** each qualification gate (`research-016`, `research-017`) for **disparate impact** on women’s typical leadership pathways (e.g. co-founder vs sole named author; unpaid movement vs salaried role).  
4. **Compare** to a deliberately expanded “shadow pipeline” (non-scored longlist) to estimate **selection vs pipeline**.

---
